elements.sent-2411-001: Coloured partition algebras were introduced by Bloss and exhibit a Schur-Weyl duality with certain complex reflection groups.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper we show that these algebras exhibit homological stability by demonstrating that their homology groups are stably isomorphic to the homology groups of a wreath product.
elements.sent-2411-003: Homological and cohomological stability are phenomena that were first observed in the study of group homology.
elements.sent-2411-004: To say that a family of groups with inclusions \begin{equation}G_0\rightarrow G_1 \rightarrow G_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow G_n \rightarrow \cdots\end{equation} satisfies homological stability is to say that the induced maps on homology \begin{equation}H_{i}(G_0) \rightarrow H_{i}(G_1)\rightarrow H_{i}(G_2)\rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow H_i(G_n) \rightarrow \cdots\end{equation} are isomorphisms of $k$-modules (where $k$ is a unital, commutative ground ring) in a range that depends on $i$ and $n$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Similarly, cohomological stability is the phenomenon that the induced maps \begin{equation}\cdots \rightarrow H^{i}(G_n) \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow H^{i}(G_2) \rightarrow H^{i}(G_1) \rightarrow H^{i}(G_0)\end{equation} are isomorphisms in such a range.
elements.sent-2411-006: Homological stability was first observed for the symmetric groups \cite{Nakaoka .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Since then, many other families of groups have been shown to satisfy homological stability (we refer the reader to \cite{RWW for a framework for homological stability that covers a long list of groups).}
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, Boyd and Hepworth have extended the machinery of homological stability to study the homology of algebras.
elements.sent-2411-009: The key observation is that the group (co)homology can be expressed as $\Tor$ and $\Ext$ over the group algebra.
elements.sent-2411-010: Explicitly, for a group $G$, let $\1$ denote the trivial $k[G]$-module: a copy of $k$ where $k[G]$ acts via the augmentation map $k[G]\rightarrow \1$ determined by $g \mapsto 1_k$ for all $g\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Using this observation, and Benson's definition of the (co)homology of augmented algebras (\cite[Definition 2.4.4]{Benson1 ), one can say the following.}
elements.sent-2411-013: A family of augmented $k$-algebras, with appropriate trivial modules and inclusion maps \begin{equation}A_0\rightarrow A_1 \rightarrow A_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow A_n \rightarrow \cdots\end{equation} satisfies (co)homological stability if the induced maps \begin{equation}\Tor_i^{A_0}(\1 , \1) \rightarrow \Tor_i^{A_1}(\1, \1) \rightarrow \Tor_i^{A_2}(\1 ,\1) \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \Tor_i^{A_n}(\1,\1) \rightarrow\cdots\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\cdots \rightarrow \Ext_{A_n}^i(\1 ,\1) \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \Ext_{A_2}^i(\1 ,\1)\rightarrow \Ext_{A_1}^i(\1 ,\1) \rightarrow \Ext_{A_0}^i(\1 ,\1)\end{equation} are isomorphisms in some range which depends $i$ and $n$.
elements.sent-2411-014: There are many interesting examples of algebras which have been shown to exhibit homological stability: the Temperley-Lieb algebras \cite{BH1,Sroka ; the Brauer algebras \cite{BHP ; Iwahori-Hecke algebras of Type $A$ and Type $B$ \cites{Hepworth, Moselle ; the partition algebras \cite{BHP2, Boyde2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A variety of methods are involved in proving such results.
elements.sent-2411-016: For the Temperley-Lieb algebras, homological stability follows by proving vanishing results (using inductive resolutions in the case of \cite{BH1 and the cellular Davis complex in the case of \cite{Sroka ).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: For the Brauer algebras and the partition algebras, homological stability is deduced from known homological stability results for the symmetric groups (using inductive resolutions in the case of \cite{BHP,BHP2 and the Mayer-Vietoris complex in the case of \cite{Boyde2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper we demonstrate that the partition diagrams coloured by a group $G$, as defined by Bloss \cite{Bloss , exhibit (co)homological stability.}
elements.sent-2411-019: For a group $G$, Bloss demonstrated that the $G$-coloured partition algebra exhibit a Schur-Weyl duality with the wreath product groups $G\wr \Sigma_n$ \cite{Bloss *{Theorem 6.6 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: For a finite group $G$, Mori \cite[Remark 4.25]{Mori shows that the $G$-coloured partition algebras arise as the endomorphism ring of a certain object in a Deligne-type category (see \cite{Deligne,Knop1,Knop2,Mori ) built from $\Rep(k[G])$, the category of $k[G]$-modules which are finitely-generated and projective over $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This is the first result in the literature on homological stability of augmented algebras to make use of the homological stability of wreath products (see \cite{Gan,HW ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our method of proof will make use of Boyde's Mayer-Vietoris complex.
elements.sent-2411-023: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-024: In Section \ref{CPA-sec , we recall the definitions of the partition algebras and coloured partition algebras.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In Section \ref{aug-alg-sec , we recall the definition of (co)homology of an augmented algebra in terms of certain $\Tor$ and $\Ext$ groups.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Section \ref{idem-cover-sec contains the most technical part of our work.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We show that a certain two-sided ideal inside the coloured partition algebra has a free idempotent left cover.
elements.sent-2411-028: This allows us to use the machinery of Boyde to deduce, in Section \ref{cohom-stab-sec , that the (co)homology of coloured partition algebras is stably isomorphic to the group (co)homology of a wreath product and, therefore, that the edge coloured partition algebras satisfy (co)homological stability.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The second author would like to thank Andrew Fisher and Sarah Whitehouse for helpful conversations in work related to this project.
elements.sent-2411-030: He would also like to thank Rachael Boyd and Richard Hepworth for interesting conversations at the 2024 British Topology Meeting in Aberdeen and Guy Boyde for helpful conversations on related work.
elements.sent-2411-031: Throughout the paper, $k$ will denote a unital, commutative ring.
elements.sent-2411-032: We will use $\ul{n}$ to denote the set $\{1, \dotsc, n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Throughout the paper, $G$ will denote a group.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this section we recall the definitions of the partition algebras and the $G$-coloured partition algebras.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, we recall these algebras as the endomorphism rings of a certain objects in a $k$-linear category.
elements.sent-2411-036: This recovers the algebras as originally defined by Jones, Martin and Bloss (\cite{Jones,Martin-partition,Bloss ).}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this subsection, we give a description of the partition category.
elements.sent-2411-038: This was introduced by Deligne \cite{Deligne and has been further studied in the papers \cite{CO,LS1,Comes .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The construction we present here is equivalent to those that exist in the literature but is framed in the language of cospans.
elements.sent-2411-040: Throughout this section we will work in the category $\mathbf{Fin}$ of finite sets and set maps.
elements.sent-2411-041: Recall that a partition of a finite set $A$ is a collection of non-empty subsets of $A$ such that each element of $A$ lies in precisely one of the subsets.
elements.sent-2411-042: We call this collection of subsets the set of components of the partition.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, recall that a cospan in the category $\mathbf{Fin}$ is a diagram of the form $A\rightarrow U \leftarrow B$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The morphisms $A\rightarrow U$ and $B\rightarrow U$ will be referred to as the left and right legs of the cospan.
elements.sent-2411-045: Two cospans $A\rightarrow U \leftarrow B$ and $A\rightarrow U^{\prime \leftarrow B$ are said to be equivalent if there is an isomorphism $U\rightarrow U^{\prime}$ compatible with the legs of the two cospans.}
elements.sent-2411-046: A partition diagram from $A$ to $B$ is a partition of $A\sqcup B$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Equivalently, it is an equivalence class of cospans $A\rightarrow U\leftarrow B$ such that both legs are surjective.
elements.sent-2411-048: The set $U$ is the set of components.
elements.sent-2411-049: Given a partition of $A\sqcup B$, we can form a cospan $A\rightarrow U\leftarrow B$ of the correct form where the legs consist of the maps sending the elements of $A$ and $B$ to the components which contain them.
elements.sent-2411-050: Conversely, given an equivalence class of cospans $A\rightarrow U\leftarrow B$, for each $u\in U$, we take the disjoint union of the preimage of $u$ in $A$ and in $B$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The collection of these sets is the necessary partition.
elements.sent-2411-052: We take a vertex for every element of $A\sqcup B$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Two vertices lie in the same component if and only if they lie in the same part of the partition.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this way we see that there are many possible representatives of a partition: the structure of the graph, beyond its components, is irrelevant.
elements.sent-2411-055: In all the diagrams we draw, we will adopt the convention that the vertices of $A$ are in a single column on the left and the vertices of $B$ are in a single column the right, and we will use language in keeping with this geometric convention (see Definition \ref{terminology-defn below).}
elements.sent-2411-057: Composition is defined by the usual recipe for composing (co)spans \cite{Benabou , which ensures associativity.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The composite of $A\rightarrow U\leftarrow B$ and $B\rightarrow V\leftarrow C$ is $A\rightarrow W\leftarrow C$, where $W$ is the union of the images of $A$ and $C$ in the pushout $U\cup_BV$.
elements.sent-2411-059: The identity on $A$ consists of identities $A\rightarrow A\leftarrow A$.
elements.sent-2411-060: When regarded as a partition, it is the partition on $A\sqcup A$ with all components of size two, each containing matching elements, one from each summand.
elements.sent-2411-061: When we compose in this way, we say that the number of internal components is the size of the subset of $U\cup_BV$ not in the image of $A$ or $C$.
elements.sent-2411-062: In terms of partitions, this amounts to the following: given partitions of $A\sqcup B$ and $B\sqcup C$, we form the finest partition of $A\sqcup B\sqcup C$ so that each part of the two given partitions is contained in some part, and then we restrict to $A\sqcup C$.
elements.sent-2411-063: The number of internal components is then the number of components omitted by this restriction: those contained entirely within $B$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We will require some terminology, to be used throughout the rest of the paper: \begin{defn Let $A$ and $B$ be finite sets and let $D$ be a partition diagram from $A$ to $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The elements of $A$ will be referred to as the vertices of $A$, and similarly, the elements of $B$ will be referred to as the vertices of $B$;.
elements.sent-2411-066: Two elements in the same part will be referred to as an edge;.
elements.sent-2411-067: A partition diagram from $A$ to $A$ consisting entirely of propagating components of size two will be called a permutation diagram.
elements.sent-2411-068: The linear partition category $\cP(\delta)$ is the $k$-linear category with objects consisting of finite sets, and homsets $\cP(\delta)(A,B)$ given by the free $k$-module with basis the set of partition diagrams from $A$ to $B$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Composition is extended linearly from the following recipe: the composite of diagrams $D_1$ and $D_2$ is $\delta^nD_1D_2$: the scalar multiple of the composite diagram $D_1D_2$ by $\delta$ raised to the power of the number of internal components in composing $D_1$ and $D_2$.
elements.sent-2411-070: For definiteness, when we are working with partition diagrams from $\ul{m}$ to $\ul{n}$ we will write $1,\ldots,m$ for elements of the left-hand summand, and $\ol{1 ,\ldots,\ol{n}$ for elements of the right-hand summand.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Categories of partitions coloured by elements of a group have been studied in \cite{LS2 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: In this subsection we describe the construction, continuing to use our terminology from the previous subsection.
elements.sent-2411-073: A $G$-colouring of a partition diagram consists of a function $\gamma$, defined on pairs of elements in the same part, and taking values in $G$, such that:.
elements.sent-2411-074: The definition of a $G$-colouring amounts exactly to a map of groupoids from the indiscrete groupoid on the partition to $G$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Indeed, any colouring can be determined by fixing an element $x$, defining $\gamma(x,y)$ for all $y\neq x$, and extending the definition to all elements using $\gamma(x,x)=1$ and $\gamma(y_1,y_2)=\gamma(x,y_1)^{-1}\gamma(x,y_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-076: A more canonical construction is to choose $\alpha_x\in G$ for each element $x$, and to define $\gamma(x,y)=\alpha_x^{-1}\alpha_y$.
elements.sent-2411-077: This construction is unchanged by taking $\alpha'_x=g\alpha_x$, so there is a set of $G$-colourings of a component naturally isomorphic to the quotient of $G^n$ by the free action of $G$ by left multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-078: The composition of $G$-coloured diagrams $D_1$ with $D_2$ is $\delta^nD_1D_2$ (where $n$ is the number of internal components) if the $G$-colourings extend to a $G$-colouring of $D_1D_2$, and zero otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is also natural to draw $G$-coloured partitions using graphs.
elements.sent-2411-080: In order to capture the $G$-colouring, they must be directed graphs, and edges must be labelled by elements of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-081: To find $\gamma(x,y)$, one multiplies the edge labels of a path from $x$ to $y$; traversing an edge labelled by $g$ in the reverse direction amounts to traversing an edge labelled by $g^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-082: We implicitly assume that, if there are multiple paths, then they will have the same product.
elements.sent-2411-083: An equivalent condition, which is easier to check, is that any loops have product $1\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Regarded in such language, a composite of partitions is zero if it would form a loop around which the product is non-trivial.
elements.sent-2411-085: In order to keep our diagrams simple, if edges are not drawn in a component, we assume that that component has the trivial $G$-colouring.
elements.sent-2411-086: We note that there is an inclusion map $P_n(\delta,G)\rightarrow P_{n+1 (\delta,G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This sends a $G$-coloured partition, $D$, of the set $\ul{n}$ to the $G$-coloured partition of the set $\ul{n+1}$ consisting of the components of $D$ and the component $\{n+1, \ol{n+1}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The $G$-colouring, $\gamma$, of $D$ is extended to this new partition by defining $\gamma(n+1,\ol{n+1 )=1\in G$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In this section we define the (co)homology of $G$-coloured partition algebras, following Benson \cite[Definition 2.4.4]{Benson1 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Recall that a $k$-algebra $A$ is said to be augmented if it comes equipped with a $k$-algebra map $\varepsilon\colon A \rightarrow k$.
elements.sent-2411-091: One can check the the composite of two coloured partition $n$-diagrams, having $i$ and $j$ propagating components respectively, has at most $\min(i,j)$ propagating components.
elements.sent-2411-092: We can therefore make the following definition.
elements.sent-2411-093: Let $I_{n-1}$ denote the two-sided ideal in $P_n(\delta , G)$ spanned $k$-linearly by non-permutation diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-094: The two-sided ideal $I_{n-1}$ has a $k$-module basis of $G$-coloured partition $n$-diagrams with fewer than $n$ propagating components.
elements.sent-2411-095: The quotient therefore has a $k$-module basis consisting of the permutation diagrams (that is, the $G$-coloured partition $n$-diagrams with precisely $n$-propagating edges).
elements.sent-2411-096: The definition of $I_{n-1}$ also allows us to make the following definition.
elements.sent-2411-097: The trivial module, $\mathbbm{1}$, consists of a single copy of the ground ring $k$, where $P_n(\delta , G)$ acts on $k$ via the augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-098: We begin by recalling the definition of $k$-free idempotent left cover from \cite{Boyde2 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Having recalled the definition, we prove that the two-sided ideal $I_{n-1}$ introduced in the previous section has a $k$-free idempotent left cover.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is the key technical work required to deduce our main result.
elements.sent-2411-101: Let $I$ be a two-sided ideal of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-103: An idempotent left cover of $I$ of height $h$ and width $w$ is a collection of left ideals $J_1,\dotsc , J_w$ in $A$ such that.
elements.sent-2411-104: If $I$ is free as a $k$-module, then an idempotent left cover is said to be $k$-free if there is a choice of $k$-basis for $I$ such that each $J_i$ is free on a subset of this basis.
elements.sent-2411-105: This in turn allows us to deduce stable isomorphisms \begin{equation}\Tor_{q}^{A}(\1,\1) \cong \Tor_{q}^{A/I}(\1,\1) \quad and \quad \Ext_A^{q}(\1,\1) \cong \Ext_{A/I}^q(\1,\1)\end{equation} for $q\leqslant h$ by \cite[Theorem 1.7]{Boyde2 and \cite[Proposition 4.2.1]{Fisher-Graves respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: This will be our plan of attack.
elements.sent-2411-107: We will construct a $k$-free idempotent left cover of the two-sided ideal $I_{n-1}$ in $P_n(\delta,G)$ and use the results mentioned above to deduce stable isomorphisms on (co)homology.
elements.sent-2411-108: We begin by defining our families of left ideals.
elements.sent-2411-109: For each $g\in G$, let $L_{i,j,g}$ denote the left ideal of $P_n(\delta ,G)$ spanned $k$-linearly by $G$-coloured partition $n$-diagrams with $\ol{i}$ and $\ol{j}$ in the same component and $\gamma(\ol{i ,\ol{j )=g$.}}

elements.sent-2411-169: We deduce that the $G$-coloured partition algebras satisfy (co)homological stability.
elements.sent-2411-170: Recall that $I_{n-1}$ is the two-sided ideal of $P_n(\delta,G)$ spanned $k$-linearly by the non-permutation diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-171: By Lemma \ref{partition-ideal-iso-lem , there is an isomorphism of $k$-algebras $P_n(\delta,G)/I_{n-1 \cong k[G\wr \Sigma_n]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Lemma \ref{height-lem tells us that the left ideals $K_i$ and $L_{i,j,g}$ form a $k$-free idempotent left cover of $I_{n-1}$ of height $n-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-173: The homological statement follows from \cite[Theorem 1.7]{Boyde2 and the cohomolocal statement follows from \cite[Proposition 4.2.2]{Fisher-Graves .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: This follows from Theorem \ref{partition-cohom-stability-thm together with Gan's result (\cite[Corollary 6]{Gan ) for the homological stability of wreath products $G\wr \Sigma_n$ (see also \cite[Proposition 1.6]{HW ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: We note that the homological stability of wreath products implies the cohomological stability by the universal coefficient theorem and the five lemma.
